coredata.dc:title: Quantum dots as ecofriendly and aqueous phase substitutes of carbon family for traditional corrosion inhibitors: A perspective
coredata.dc:creator: Verma, Chandrabhan
Alfantazi, Akram
Quraishi, M.A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) or quantum dots (QDs) are a new and rising member of the carbon family that has withdrawn substantial academic and industrial attention. Unlike to most of the carbon allotropes such as CNTs, graphene and fullerene, CDs are associated with numerous advantages including substantial biocompatibility, high quantum yield, magnified adsorption ability, excellent electric conductivity, low toxicity, cost-effectivity, eco-friendliness, high solubility and excellent stability. Because of their ecofriendly and high solubility, recently CDs are being used as aqueous phase corrosion inhibitors. Literature inspection suggests that CDs derived from citric acid, ammonium citrate, urea, L-histidine and thiourea exhibit remarkable anticorrosive activity. Spectroscopic techniques such as SEM, TEM, HRTEM, AFM, FT-IR, UV–vis, EDX and XPS are mainly employed to characterize the developed CDs. Literature outcomes suggest that N doped, N,S co-doped and chemically modified CDs exhibit higher anticorrosive activity than purified CDs. The CDs mostly behave as mixed- and interface-type inhibitors. Adsorption of CDs on metal surfaces mostly followed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Surface investigations principally SEM, AFM, EDX and XPS analyses are widely conducted to demonstrate the corrosion inhibition using CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon allotropes
Nanomaterials
Surface characterization
Mixed-type and Langmuir adsorption isotherm

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dots: Basics and anticorrosive applications
Carbon dots as aqueous phase corrosion inhibitors: Literature survey
CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper
CDs as corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel
CDs as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Properties and current industrial and biological applications of carbon dots.
Common carbon allotropes and their dimensional specification [11].

3D AFM images (left side) and corresponding height profile of (a) polished copper surface, (b) corroded in 0.5 M H2SO4 in the absence of N,S-CDs and (c) corroded in 0.5 M H2SO4 in the presence of 50 ppm N,S-CDs [18].
High resolution XPS spectra of copper substrate for (b) Cu 2p, (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) O 1 s and (f) S 2p [18].

High resolution (a) Cu 2p, (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s and (d) XPS spectra of corrosion products and 3D images of (e) corroded copper surface in 0.5 M H2SO4 without N-CDs and (f) corroded copper surface in 0.5 M H2SO4 with 200 ppm of N-CDs [19].
(a) Nyquist, (b) Bode frequency, (c) Bode Phase angle and (d) Tafel polarization plots for Q235 CS corrosion in 1 M HCl in the absence and presence of different concentrations of N doped CDs [25].

SEM micrographs of (a-b) polished Q235 CS surface, (c-d) Q235 CS corroded in 1 M HCl without CDs, (e-f) Q235 CS corroded in 1 M HCl with 1 mg/L CDs and (g-h) Q235 CS corroded in 1 M HCl with 1 mg/L CDs [25].

Current density for Q235 CS corrosion (a) in 1 M HCl-24 h, (b) in 1 M HCl + 25 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h, (c) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h, (d) in 3.5% NaCl-24 h, (e) in 3.5% NaCl + 25 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h and (f) in 3.5% NaCl + 200 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h [26].

3D AFM images of Q235 CS steel corroded in (a) 1 M HCl + 0 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h, (b) 1 M HCl + 25 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h, (c) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h, (d) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L IM-CDs-48 h, (e) 3.5% NaCl + 0 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h, (f) (e) 3.5% NaCl + 25 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h, (g) (e) 3.5% NaCl + 200 mg/L IM-CDs-24 h and (h) (e) 3.5% NaCl + 200 mg/L IM-CDs-48 h [26].
Nyquist (left), Bode frequency (middle) and Bode phase angle (right) plots for Q235 CS corrosion in 1 M HCl in the absence and presence of (a-c) CA-CDs, (d-f) imidazole and (g-i) FCDs [27].

Current density distributions for Q235 CS corrosion in various condition and time; (a) 1 M HCl + 0%-12 h, (b) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L CA-CDs-12 h, (c) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L imidazole-12 h, (d) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L FCDs-12 h, (e) 1 M HCl + 100 mg/L CA-FCDs-12 h and (f) (b) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L CA-FCDs-24 h [27].

SEM (upper) and AFM images for Q235 CS corrosion in various condition and time; (a) 1 M HCl + 0%-12 h, (b) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L CA-CDs-12 h, (c) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L imidazole-12 h, (d) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L FCDs-12 h, (e) 1 M HCl + 100 mg/L CA-FCDs-12 h and (f) (b) 1 M HCl + 200 mg/L CA-FCDs-24 h [27].
SEM (left side) and AFM (right side) micrographs of (a) polished MS surface, (b) MS surface corroded in 15% HCl without N,S-CDs and (c) MS surface corroded in 15% HCl with N,S-CDs [30].
A summary of corrosion inhibition potential of CDs for copper, carbon steel (CS) and mild steel (MS).

row.entry: Pyromelletic acid with thiourea, urea and DETA

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: GBCP: Green bean coffee powder, ASA: Aminosalicylic acid, DETA: Diethylenetriamine

head.ce:title: Quantum dots as ecofriendly and aqueous phase substitutes of carbon family for traditional corrosion inhibitors: A perspective

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Research Center for Advanced Materials, King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals, Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Research Center for Advanced Materials
King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Interdisciplinary Research Center for Advanced Materials, King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals, Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 2533, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Khalifa University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 2533, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) or quantum dots (QDs) are a new and rising member of the carbon family that has withdrawn substantial academic and industrial attention. Unlike to most of the carbon allotropes such as CNTs, graphene and fullerene, CDs are associated with numerous advantages including substantial biocompatibility, high quantum yield, magnified adsorption ability, excellent electric conductivity, low toxicity, cost-effectivity, eco-friendliness, high solubility and excellent stability. Because of their ecofriendly and high solubility, recently CDs are being used as aqueous phase corrosion inhibitors. Literature inspection suggests that CDs derived from citric acid, ammonium citrate, urea, L-histidine and thiourea exhibit remarkable anticorrosive activity. Spectroscopic techniques such as SEM, TEM, HRTEM, AFM, FT-IR, UV–vis, EDX and XPS are mainly employed to characterize the developed CDs. Literature outcomes suggest that N doped, N,S co-doped and chemically modified CDs exhibit higher anticorrosive activity than purified CDs. The CDs mostly behave as mixed- and interface-type inhibitors. Adsorption of CDs on metal surfaces mostly followed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Surface investigations principally SEM, AFM, EDX and XPS analyses are widely conducted to demonstrate the corrosion inhibition using CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots: Basics and anticorrosive applications
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to traditional heterocyclic corrosion inhibitors, CDs are also become effective by adsorbing and forming hydrophobic protective film. Theoretically, in doped CDs heteroatoms are expected to act as active sites for adsorption process as they are rich in electrons. Therefore, doped CDs should behave as superior corrosion inhibitors as compared to undoped CDs [15]. Obviously, this type of doping also helps CDs in getting them solubilized in the polar electrolytes. It can be assumed that after getting adsorbed, CDs block the active sites present on the metallic surfaces, which are responsible for corrosion. Adsorption mechanism of corrosion inhibition using CDs can be illustrated using electrochemical (PDP and EIS) and surface investigation methods. SEM, AFM, FT-IR, UV–vis, EDX, XPS and XRD are the common techniques that have been employed in determining the anticorrosive property of CDs. Obviously, through these methods change in surface morphology and change in surface elemental composition can be observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots as aqueous phase corrosion inhibitors: Literature survey
CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper
ce:section.ce:para: Copper and its alloys are widely used for different industrial applications, especially for instruments of marine applications. However, these materials are exceedingly liable for corrosive damage. The implementation of organic molecules has been identified as the first line of defense against copper corrosion in marine environments. CDs are a new member of the carbon family and because of their environmental friendly nature and ability to dissolve in polar electrolytes, their employment for different industrial and biological applications are growing rapidly. Their aqueous phase anticorrosive application is also not behind. Literature inspection suggests that CDs, especially doped with nitrogen, are widely used as corrosion inhibitors for various metals. The inhibition effect of CDs derived from durian for copper corrosion in 1% NaCl solution can be regarded as the first report on the anticorrosive effect of CDs [16]. Synthesis of CDs was characterized using UV–visible, IR and fluorescence spectroscope. IR investigation suggests that CDs surface contains various electron rich polar functional groups such as –OH (hydroxyl), >C = S (thiocarbonyl) and > C = O (carbonyl) which solubilize CDs in marine solution and act as adsorption sites. Studied CDs showed the highest efficiency of 86% at 800 ppm. PDP study informs that CDs retard both anodic and cathodic reactions and demonstrate themselves as mixed-type inhibitors. Later on corrosion inhibition of CDs derived from urea & GBCP were employed as an anticorrosive material for copper/1% NaCl system [17]. Similar observations were observed.

Recently, Zhang and coworkers [18] developed N and S co-doped CDs, designated N,S-CDs, having as high as 17% and 19% of N and S content, respectively. N,S-CDs characterization was achieved using SEM, FT-IR, TEM, Raman, AFM and XPS methods. PDP and EIS studies were carried out to demonstrate the inhibition potential of N,S-CDs. Using PDP, it was established that N,S-CDs act as a mixed-type inhibitor, however slightly cathodic predominance. Electrochemical analyses demonstrate the N,S-CDs become effectual by building corrosion protective film. Numerous surface investigating methods were employed to describe N,S-CDs adsorption. SEM study conducted for 24 h immersion with and without optimum (50 ppm) concentration of N,S-CDs reveals that they effectively inhibit copper corrosion because surface morphology was greatly improved in the presence of N,S-CDs as compared to polished and corroded metal surface in N,S-CDs absence. Smoothness in the surface morphology using N,S-CDs and their adsorption ability corrosion mitigation was also reinforced by AFM study. AFM images and corresponding height profile of polished and corroded metal surfaces with and without N,S-CDs are presented in Fig. 3 . It can be clearly seen that the AFM image of polished metal surface is very smooth with average roughness (Ra) of only 4.806 nm. The copper surface becomes highly roughed and corroded when allowed to corrode in the absence of N,S-CDs. This is due to free aggressive attacks by electrolyte molecules in the absence of N,S-CDs. In this situation, corroded metal surfaces acquire Ra value of 40.893 nm. Nevertheless, morphology of corroded surface in the presence of N,S-CDs is greatly improved (Ra = 15.363 nm) due to the formation of inhibitive film.

Shift in the IR frequencies of adsorbed N,S-CDs as compared to the purified N,S-CDs also validated the adsorption approach of corrosion defense. XPS analysis provides most significant insight about corrosion mitigation using the adsorption approach. XPS spectrum of adsorbed N,S-CDs furnishes five intense signals, corresponding to Cu 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and S 2p (Fig. 4 ). It can be seen that the XPS spectrum of Cu 2p gives two signals at 932.5 eV and 952.3 eV for Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, respectively. This observation indicates the presence of Cu (I). The Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 signals for Cu(II) would appear at 943 eV and 963 eV, respectively. A most intense XPS signal at 2.84.8 eV (Fig. 4c) is due to C–C bond, though some less intense signals that may correspond to C–N, C–O and C = O was also observed. In N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4d), a signal at 401.4 eV corresponds to the N; Cu component. The Q 1 s spectrum peak at around 532.2 eV corresponds to Cu2O. And finally, a highly intense peak at 162.5 eV (Fig. 4f) in XPS spectrum of S 2p is attributed due to Cu-S. Based on these observations authors proposed that N,S-CDs effectively adsorb and inhibit corrosion.

Similar observation was derived by authors elsewhere [19], where corrosion inhibition potential of N doped CDs (N-CDs) was studied for copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 . Synthesis of N-CDs, AFM images copper surface corroded in 0.5 M H2SO4 and high resolution Cu 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s spectra of corrosion products are illustrated in Fig. 5 . AFM spectral analysis suggests that corroded copper surface is highly rough having Ra of 73.042 nm which reduces to 22.146 nm in the presence of 200 ppm concentration of N-CDs. Smoothness in the surface morphology in the presence of N-CDs validates the corrosion mitigation through their adsorption. XPS spectra of corrosion products show similar patterns as described by these authors before [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their high mechanical supremacy, toughness, long-life and cost-effectiveness, carbon steel alloys are comprehensively engaged as construction materials. Nevertheless, their life cut short because of their ability to corrode in the presence of an aggressive environment. The corrosion problems become more pronounced during acid cleaning processes of steel alloys where a gigantic quantity of metal component is vanished. Therefore, these cleaning practices exploit outside additives to avoid or minimize the detrimental metallic loss. Obviously, the expenditure of organic inhibitors is the most effective and peculiar method of corrosion mitigation in such conditions. In the last four years, the use of CDs as anticorrosive materials for carbon steel is acquiring attention [20–24]. Cui et al [25] investigated anticorrosive activity of N doped CDs derived from ASA precursor for Q235 CS/ 1 M HCl system. CDs attain aptitude to restrain both anodic and cathodic Tafel reactions devoid of drastic shift in E corr. Through this outcome mixed-type behavior of CDs was established. More so, CDs cause noticeable boost in the value of charge transfer (Rct) for corrosion as augment in the width of Nyquist curves was assessed with CDs.

This suggests that CDs acts as a interface-type inhibitors i.e. CDs build hydrophobic and protective film at Q235 CS and 1 M HCl interface. This argument was also supported by enlargement in the degree of phase angle in inhibited Bode plots. Obviously, enlargement in phase angle values indicated improvement in the morphology of protected surfaces. The enlargement in the diameter of Nyquist curves and phase angle value of Bode plots is dependable with the concentration of N doped CDs. Tafel polarization, Nyquist and Bode (phase angle and frequency) plots for Q235 CS corrosion in 1 M HCl are shown in Fig. 6 . Adsorption of N doped CDs was also supported by SEM study. 2D micrographs of Q235 steel corroded in 1MHCl for 88 h in different experimental conditions at 100 X and 200 X magnifications are shown in Fig. 7 . From Fig. 7a-b it can be seen that polished Q235 CS surfaces are highly smooth with some cracks on them. However, after allowing them to corrode in 1 M HCl, the surfaces are extremely damaged with mountain like appearance and cracks. This might be recognized by the free acid corrosion in the absence of CDs. Further, morphology of the Q235 CS surfaces damaged in 1 M HCl with 1 mg/L concentration of CDs are similar to that of the non-protected one. This outcome revealed that CDs are not effective at the lower (1 mg/L) concentration. However, at 5 mg/L concentration surface morphologies smoothened remarkably.

Yang and coworkers [26] describe the surface functionalization of CDs using imidazole (IM-CDs) and its subsequent anticorrosive application for Q235 CS/ 1MHCl and 3.5% NaCl systems. Synthesis of CDs from citric acid and corresponding surface functionalization is illustrated in Fig. 8 . Synthesized IM-CDs were characterized using FT-IR, UV–vis, Raman and XPS spectral analyses. IM-CDs act as effective anticorrosive materials in both electrolytes. Through polarization study it was derived that IM-CDs exhibit 92.60% and 83.45% efficiency in 1 M HCl and 3.5% NaCl solution, respectively at 200 ppm.

Scanning vibrating electrode technology (SVET) measurement which provides information about the local corrosion situation was adopted to demonstrate the adsorption behavior of IM-CDs. The local corrosion results for Q235 corrosion in 1 M HCl and 3.5% NaCl electrolytes in the absence and presence of IM-CDs are given in Fig. 9 . Obviously, a high positive current indicates the condition of high anodic dissolution or corrosion. From Fig. 9a it can be seen that Q235 CS corrosion in 1 M HCl acquires the highest positive current identifying that in this situation Q235 steel surface is extremely corroded. However, in the presence of IM-CDs at 25 mg/L and 200 mg/L concentration, the current shifted towards negative directions. This observation indicates that IM-CDs decrease the local current through the formation of protective film. Likewise, for Q235 CS corrosion in 3.5% NaCl current density was around 0.00 (μA cm−2) in the absence of IM-CDs. Nevertheless, the current density shifted in the negative direction in the presence of 25 mg/L and 200 mg/L concentration of IM-CDs.

The adsorption mechanism of corrosion prevention was also supported by AFM study in which Q235 CS surface were allowed to corrode for 24 h and/or 48 h in the absence and presence of IM-CDs. 3D AFM images are illustrated in Fig. 10 . It can be seen that the metal surface is corroded badly in 1 M HCl and 3.5% NaCl without IM-CDs. However, the presence of 25 mg/L and 200 mg/L concentration of IM-CDs causes significant smoothness in the surface morphology. This smoothness was more pronounced at higher IM-CDs concentration as in HCl system, Ra values of Q235 CS surface corroded for 24 h in the presence of 25 mg/L and 200 mg/L were 0.791 μm and 0.185 μm, respectively. Same trend was followed for Q235 CS corrosion in 3.5% NaCl.

In another study Ye et al. [27] developed functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) using imidazole and citric acid and tested them as anticorrosive materials for Q235 CS in 1MHCl. A relative anticorrosive order of CA-CDs (citric acid carbon dots), imidazole and functionalized CDs (FCDs) was demonstrated using electrochemical and surface demonstrations. The inhibition efficiency of the imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs followed the sequence (EIS/PDP studies): FCDs (94.0% at 200 ppm). > imidazole (83.2% at 200 ppm). > CA-CDs (38% at 200 ppm). It was also observed that increase in imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs concentration causes subsequent increase in the diameter of Nyquist curves thereby charge transfer process. The presence of imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs in corrosive medium of 1 M HCl results in decrease in overall current density by suppressing either of Tafel reactions. This increase in Nyquist curves diameter and decrease in polarization current density was consistent with their order of effectiveness and concentration. The increase in diameter of Nyquist curves is due to the formation of protective film by imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs at the interface which enhances the barrier for charge transfer. The decrease in current density indicates that the imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs block the surface active sites through their adsorption. Imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs did not shift Ecorr value drastically indicating that they behave as mixed-type inhibitors. The increase in phase angle value of inhibited Bode plots than that of the non-inhibited one is due to smoothening of the inhibited surface through imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs adsorption. Nyquist and Bode plots for Q235 CS corrosion in 1 M HCl with and without imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs are shown in Fig. 11 .

Adsorption of imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs on metal surface and subsequent corrosion protection was also demonstrated using SVET study. It can be clearly seen that highest positive current was obtained in the case of Q235 CS corrosion in 1 M HCl. In the presence of imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs the current density shifted in negative direction and this shift is more pronounced in the presence of various concentrations of FCDs. Noticeably, current density for uninhibited and inhibited by CA-CDs systems are almost similar indicating that CA-CDs does not exhibit remarkable anticorrosive activity. However, in the remaining situations, current densities are remarkably shifted. The current density distribution for Q235 CS corrosion in 1 M HCl in various conditions is shown in Fig. 12 .

Lastly, adsorption of imidazole, CA-CDs and FCDs on Q235 CS steel was also supported by SEM and AFM analyses. For these investigations, Q235 CS surfaces are allowed to corrode in 1 M HCl for 24 h and/ or 48 h in the absence and presence of 200 ppm concentration of imidazole and CA-CDs and 100 ppm and 200 ppm concentration of FCDs. The inspection of SEM images suggests that morphologies of the uninhibited Q235 CS surface and inhibited by CA-CDs and imidazole are comparable. However, in the presence of FCDs, especially at 200 ppm concentration, Q235 CS surface morphology was greatly smoothened. These observations indicate that FCDs is much more effective anticorrosive material than CA-CDs and imidazole. More so, SEM morphology of FCDs protected Q235 surfaces after 24 h and 48 h are similar without any appreciable change which suggests that FCDs can be used as a long term corrosion inhibitor. Similar inspection can be assessed using AFM study. These authors reported similar observations with CDs [28,29]. Fig. 13.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel
ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel (MS) is another industrially imperative alloy of iron which has been used widely for various household and industrial applications. Corrosion inhibition of MS is of enormous importance. Literature investigation suggests that recently some reports describing corrosion inhibition of CDs for mild steel have been documented. They exhibit more than 90% inhibition efficiency at relatively low concentration. Saraswat and Yadav [15] reported the synthesis, characterization and inhibition effect of N, S co-doped (CD1) and N doped (CD2) for mild steel in 15% HCl solution. The synthesis of CD1 and CD2 was achieved by solvothermal action of in the presence of pyromelletic acid with thiourea, urea and DETA at 180°. Various spectral techniques including TEM, FT-IR and Raman spectroscope were used to characterized CD1 and CD2. As expected, using weight loss and electrochemical methods it was assessed that at the optimum concentration (100 ppm) CD1 (S, N co-doped) exhibits relatively high inhibition efficiency than CD2 (N doped). Polarization investigation showed CD1 and CD2 acted as mixed-type behavior i.e. CD1 and CD2 cause slight shift in the value of Ecorr with respect to the black. EIS study suggests that CD1 and CD2 increase diameter of Nyquist curves identifying that they behave as interface-type inhibitors. SEM, XPS and AFM studies were conducted to show the corrosion inhibition using adsorption.

Recently, Saraswat and Yadav [30], develop a N, S co-doped (N, S-CDs) by a hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, isoniazid and thiourea for 6 h at 180 °C and used them as corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in 15% HCl solution. The synthesized N,S-CDs was characterized using various spectroscopic methods such as HRTEM, FT-IR, UV–vis, photoluminescence (PL) and XPS. Anticorrosion tendency of N,S-CDs was studied using electrochemical and surface investigations. Electrochemical studies suggested that N,S-CDs behaved as a mixed-type inhibitor and it has marked adverse effect on Tafel reactions. The decrease in current density in the presence of N,S-CDs identified that it become effective through blocking the active sites responsible for the corrosive damage. Adsorption of N,S-CDs on metal surface and subsequent effect on corrosion inhibition was also studied by SEM, XPS and AFM analyses. SEM and AFM micrographs of MS surfaces corroded in 15% HCl with and without N,S-CDs are shown in Fig. 14 . It can be seen that in the presence of N,S-CDs surface morphology of metal surface is greatly smoothened due to building of corrosion protective film. A summary of anticorrosive effect of CDs for copper, CS and MS corrosion in represented in Table 1 .

From the present discussion it has been established that carbon dots (CDs) are new and rising anticorrosive materials of the carbon family. Literature study suggests that CDs in their purified and doped form exhibit remarkably high anticorrosive activity however literature on this topic is relatively scarce. Therefore, there are extreme demands of exploring CDs as anticorrosive materials. They generally show remarkably high activity at relatively concentration. In view of the high potential, the use of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs should also be explored for other metals and alloys such as zinc, brass and aluminum. Literature survey reveals that CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs derived from citric acid, ammonium citrate, urea, histidine and thiourea are used as corrosion inhibitors. It has also been derived that N,S- co-doped CDs show comparatively higher inhibition potential than N doped CDs. In a study, imidazole functionalized CDs were evaluated as effective corrosion inhibitors. Anticorrosive use of functionalized CDs using organic compounds, especially heterocyclic compounds should also be explored. They are expected to exhibit relatively more potential than pure CDs. The CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs are mostly characterized using spectroscopic techniques such as TEM, FT-IR,SEM, HRTEM, UV–vis, EDX, AFM and XPS. The CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs become effective by adsorbing and building corrosion protective film and their adsorption mostly follows the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model. SEM, AFM, EDX and XPS methods are widely used to demonstrate the adsorption of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs on metal surfaces. The CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs act as mixed-type inhibitors. Lastly, because of their nanomeric nature, CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs are associated with huge surface area therefore they can also be evaluated as anticorrosive coating materials.

Chandrabhan Verma, Akram Alfantazi, M. A. Quraishi collectively conceptualized and designed the work and were part of the manuscript write-up. All the authors collectively wrote and contributed to the entire manuscript and have agreed to its publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
